narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways but faced challenges from your academic record and study plans. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 56.9% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, compared against thousands of past students. The biggest influence was your choice to attend part-time, which is uncommon—only about 7% of students do this—and the model sees it as a strong risk for bar failure, likely due to divided focus between work and studies pushing your odds downward. Next, your third-year school decile of 3, well below the typical 5.5 average, signaled weaker performance in later undergrad years and further tilted the prediction toward failure. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, though under the 3.2 average, actually helped your case a bit by nudging the model toward expecting success despite other red flags. Your LSAT score of 27, far below the usual 37, and first-year decile of 4 also played roles but weren't the top drivers here. Overall, your profile compares unfavorably to most applicants who pass the bar, especially full-time students with stronger deciles around the middle of their classes. While this rejection stings, it's not the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score significantly, as it weighs heavily in these models. Switching to a full-time track could also flip the script, showing the commitment that predicts bar success. Building your upper-level grades through post-bac courses or targeted prep might lift those deciles too. Reach out to admissions for feedback, and explore bridge programs or other schools with holistic reviews that value your unique path. You've got potential; refining these areas could open doors soon.
